source_metadata.title_raw: Gary R. Ray - A-18-110 - July 11, 2018 - Salinas

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 11, 2018

Gary R. Ray
Ottone Leach & Ray, LLP
295 Main Street, Suite 600
Salinas, CA 93901

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-110

Dear Mr. Ray:

This letter responds to your request on behalf of Ms. Iwamoto, the Monterey County
Cannabis Program Manager, for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest. Additionally, we are not providing advice under Section 1090, et seq.²

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Ms. Iwamoto, the Monterey County Cannabis Program Manager, act in
her private role as a trustee and beneficiary of a trust to sell real property where the sale is
contingent upon the County's approval of a conditional use permit to allow cultivation of cannabis
on the property?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² In any event, an analysis under Section 1090 is not necessary as the Act prohibits Ms. Iwamoto from playing
any role related to the permit decision, as discussed below.

File No. A-18-110
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit her actions as a trustee in the sale of the property. As a beneficiary
of the trust, Ms. Iwamoto has a prohibited financial interest in the County's permit decision and she
may not make, participate in making, or influence the permit decision.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

Ms. Iwamoto is employed by the County of Monterey as its Cannabis Program Manager
(Program Manager II) in the County Administrative Office ("CAO"). She reports to the Assistant
County Administrative Officer. No employees report directly to her. Ms. Iwamoto's position is a
point of contact for program management functions. In this position, Ms. Iwamoto's role is to
develop and manage the Cannabis Program through coordination of key departments, state and
federal agencies, as well as public and community groups. Her duties include: developing,
managing, analyzing and evaluating the Cannabis Program; making policy recommendations; and
interpreting and analyzing applicable state, federal, local and internal rules and regulations in
coordination with the Board of Supervisors, Board committees, staff, the public, stakeholders and
the regulated community.

The County's Resource Management Agency ("RM Agency") is responsible for issuing use
permits of the type needed by the buyer of the property to cultivate cannabis, based on the
appropriateness of use in compliance with the County's zoning ordinances. Per County Ordinance,
cannabis cultivation is presently limited to Farmland (F) and Industrial zoning areas, and must be
cultivated in a greenhouse or warehouse that was in existence prior to 2016. The County does not
limit the number of cannabis cultivation permits. There are no additional requirements by the
County for an applicant to make improvements in the nature of a public benefit. The permit
application is heard before the Planning Commission at a public hearing, and a denial may be
appealed to the Board of Supervisors. The RM Agency's director is appointed by the CAO, subject
to the Board of Supervisors approval. Ms. Iwamoto has no direct or indirect reporting relationship
to, or responsibilities under, the RM Agency.

In her role as the County's Cannabis Program Manager, Ms. Iwamoto does not review,
recommend, or provide any type of input regarding applications for use permits, including use
permits to cultivate cannabis. She does not have any oversight or decision-making authority over
the issuance of any use permits, including use permits to cultivate cannabis. You state that in this
role, she does not have the authority to influence any decisions regarding the issuance of any use
permits, including use permits to cultivate cannabis; and does not oversee compliance with the
requirements of any use permits including use permits to cultivate cannabis.

In her private capacity, Ms. Iwamoto is the trustee of and a beneficiary with a 100 percent
lifetime interest, in an "exemption trust."³ The Trust has a one-third ownership interest in certain

³ Exemption trusts are a form of bypass trust (bypassing the surviving spouse and passed to the children upon
the spouse's death) limited to an amount no larger than the personal federal estate tax exemption for the year of death;
the personal exemption protects this property from the estate tax. Additional property is directed to the surviving
spouse, and is shielded by the marital deduction. (See https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/exemption_trust, as of June 6,
2018.) Your law partner and Ms. Iwamoto's estate planning counsel, Ms. Leach, confirmed in a subsequent email that
this is a trust established for estate planning purposes and not for a business or profit-making purpose.

File No. A-18-110
Page No. 3

real property located in the County. She has no interests in the trusts that own the other two-third
interest in the property. Although she is the income beneficiary of the Trust, she is not presently
receiving income because the real property at issue is not rented. She has elected to sell the property
and will have the right to receive income from the Trust resulting from the sale.

The Trust is in negotiations for the sale of the property to an unrelated third party. The buyer
is purchasing the property under its business name for purposes of a commercial medical cannabis
business. Ms. Iwamoto has no interest in the buyer or the buyer's business and no relationship with
the buyer outside this purchase. The sale of the property is contingent on the buyer's ability to
obtain a conditional use permit from the County, and other required permits from the appropriate
government agencies, to allow the buyer to cultivate cannabis on the property.⁴ You provided the
following additional information in subsequent emails: the property is located within the Farmlands
Zoning (F/40) District, and has existing greenhouses and warehouses. The buyer's application is for
two indoor medium cultivations and two mixed-light medium cultivations.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has
an interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103.)

Financial Interest

Interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103, and the
following are implicated here:

• An interest in any real property where the official has a direct or indirect interest
worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income,
which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c).)⁵

Ms. Iwamoto has a financial interest in the real property at issue, due to her 100 percent
lifetime beneficial interest in the Trust. An "interest in real property" includes a pro rata share of
real property held in a trust, in which the official owns, directly or indirectly or beneficially, a 10

⁴ Pursuant to the County's "Cannabis Permitting Information" web page, the County requires all cannabis
businesses to obtain a use permit from the RM Agency; a Commercial Cannabis Business Permit from the RMA Agency
Title; and a business license from the County Treasurer. (See http://www.co.monterey.ca.us/government/departments-i-
z/resource-management-agency-rma/planning/faq-cannabis-permitting-information as of June 26, 2018; and see
Monterey County Code Title 21, Ch. 21.67 (commercial cannabis activities) and Title 7, Ch. 7.90 (commercial cannabis
permits).)

⁵ "Income" includes a pro rata share of the income of any trust in which the official owns directly, indirectly,
or beneficially, a 10 percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)

File No. A-18-110
Page No. 4

percent interest or greater. (Section 82033.) An official has a "direct, indirect or beneficial interest"
in a trust where she is a beneficiary and either presently receives income; or has an irrevocable
future right to income or principal from the trust. (Regulation 18234(c)(2).) For the future right to
be irrevocable, the right must not be limited or reduced as described in Regulation
18234(c)(2)(B)(i) and (ii).⁶ You have confirmed by email that Ms. Iwamoto has an irrevocable
future right to the Trust's income or principal, and that neither limitation in Regulation
18234(c)(2)(B) is applicable. Additionally, we note that she has a financial interest in any sources
of income to the Trust. Following the sale, she will have a financial interest in the buyer as a source
of income.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Pursuant to Regulation 18701(a) and Regulation 18702(a)(5), a real property interest is
explicitly involved in a decision and the effect of the decision on the interest is both foreseeable and
material if the decision:

Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or any
variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on, that real
property.

(Regulation 18702.2(a)(5).)

The decision at issue is the County's approval or disapproval of a conditional use permit to
allow cannabis cultivation on the property. Accordingly, Ms. Iwamoto's real property interest is
explicitly involved in the decision and the effect is both reasonably foreseeable and material. She
may not make, participate in making, or use her official position to influence that decision.

Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a Governmental Decision

Regulation 18704 defines these actions as follows:

(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision if the
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency.

(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive
review.

⁶ Regulation 18234(c)(2) (B) states: "Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal if the trust is
irrevocable, unless one of the following applies: (i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powers are not limited by an ascertainable standard relating to
the health, education, support, or maintenance of the beneficiaries; or (ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other
than the official can designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income."

File No. A-18-110
Page No. 5

(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public official
uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he or she:

(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose
of affecting a decision; or

(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the
purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact.

The facts indicate that the decision on the conditional use permit is solely under the
authority of the County's RM Agency, and that Ms. Iwamoto as the County's Cannabis Program
Manager is not in a position to make, or participate in making, this decision. The relevant
prohibition then, is that she may not use her position to influence the decision. Due to the CAO's
authority over the RM Agency, Ms. Iwamoto may not contact, or appear before, any official in the
CAO or the RM Agency for the purpose of affecting the decision on the conditional use permit.
(Regulation 18704(c)(1).) Nor may she contact, or appear before, any other official in any other
government agency for the purpose of influencing the decision, if she purports to act within her
authority as a County official in doing so. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) You note that the sale is
contingent on "other required permits from the appropriate government agencies." The prohibition
in Regulation 18704(c)(2) would apply to these decisions as well.

Regulation 18704(d)(2)(A) provides that an official may make an appearance as a member
of the general public when the matter relates solely to real property owned entirely by the official or
by the official and members of her immediate family. However, the Trust owns only one-third of
the real property at issue, and this exception is not applicable.

Please note, Ms. Iwamoto may require additional advice from our office in the future,
related to her anticipated financial interest in the buyer as a source of income following the sale.
Moreover, we analyze only a potential decision by the RM Agency regarding a permit. We express
no opinion regarding any decisions before Ms. Iwamoto as the Monterey County Cannabis Program
Manager and the potential effect of these decisions on Ms. Iwamoto's interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

percent interest or greater. (Section 82033.) An official has a "direct, indirect or beneficial interest"

QUESTION Under the Act, may Ms. Iwamoto, the Monterey County Cannabis Program Manager, act in her private role as a trustee and beneficiary of a trust to sell real property where the sale is contingent upon the County's approval of a conditional use permit to allow cultivation of cannabis on the property? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² In any event, an analysis under Section 1090 is not necessary as the Act prohibits Ms. Iwamoto from playing any role related to the permit decision, as discussed below. File No. A-18-110 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION The Act does not prohibit her actions as a trustee in the sale of the property. As a beneficiary of the trust, Ms. Iwamoto has a prohibited financial interest in the County's permit decision and she may not make, participate in making, or influence the permit decision. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR Ms. Iwamoto is employed by the County of Monterey as its Cannabis Program Manager (Program Manager II) in the County Administrative Office ("CAO"). She reports to the Assistant County Administrative Officer. No employees report directly to her. Ms. Iwamoto's position is a point of contact for program management functions. In this position, Ms. Iwamoto's role is to develop and manage the Cannabis Program through coordination of key departments, state and federal agencies, as well as public and community groups.
